content.text: Every container that runs in a Kubernetes cluster starts from an OCI image — a standardized, content-addressed bundle of filesystem layers and runtime metadata that any conforming container runtime can pull and execute. Understanding the OCI image format is foundational to understanding how Kubernetes schedules workloads, why image pulls behave as they do, and how to build images that are secure, reproducible, and fast to deploy.
The Open Container Initiative (OCI) is a Linux Foundation project that standardizes two specifications: the
(what an image contains and how it is stored) and the

(how a runtime turns an image into a running container). Before OCI, Docker's proprietary image format was the de facto standard; the OCI specification formalized it into a vendor-neutral standard that any runtime — containerd, CRI-O, Podman — can implement.
An OCI image is a directory of JSON and tar files stored in a content-addressable layout. The top-level entry point is the

— a JSON document that lists the image's configuration blob and its ordered list of filesystem layers. Every piece of the image — manifest, config, and each layer — is identified by its SHA-256 digest of its content, making the entire structure immutable and verifiable: if the digest matches, the bytes are correct.

Registries (Docker Hub, Google Artifact Registry, Amazon ECR, GitHub Container Registry) store and serve OCI images. When Kubernetes schedules a Pod to a node, the kubelet instructs the node's container runtime to pull the image from the configured registry if it is not already cached. The runtime unpacks the layers, mounts them using the host kernel's overlay filesystem, and starts the container process on top.

Before a standardized image format existed, Docker images were tied to the Docker daemon. Other runtimes had to reverse-engineer the format to achieve compatibility. This created a fragile ecosystem: a image built with

might not run on a runtime that wasn't Docker. Kubernetes, which supports pluggable runtimes through the Container Runtime Interface (CRI), required a portable image format that every CRI-compliant runtime could handle identically.

The OCI image format solves this by specifying a precise layout that decouples the image from any particular runtime implementation. The same image artifact stored in a registry can be pulled and executed by containerd on one node, CRI-O on another, and Podman on a developer's laptop — all producing identical container processes. This portability is what makes Kubernetes' multi-node, multi-runtime architecture viable.

The content-addressable design also matters for correctness and security. Because the manifest, config, and every layer are identified by their SHA-256 digest, any tampering — modifying a layer after it was published — produces a different digest, which the runtime detects and rejects. This gives Kubernetes a verifiable chain from the image name in a manifest to the bytes that actually run as a container.
Any OCI-compliant runtime can pull and run any OCI-compliant image. Kubernetes clusters can mix runtimes without compatibility concerns.
Content-addressed layers are stored once per node regardless of how many images reference them. A node running 20 images that all share the same base OS layer only stores that layer once.
), only the layers that changed need to be downloaded. Unchanged base layers are already cached on the node.
Every component is identified by its SHA-256 digest. The runtime verifies each digest during pull, detecting corruption or tampering before the container ever starts.
) guarantees the exact same bytes on every pull, forever — no tag mutation risk.

An OCI Image Index (manifest list) can reference platform-specific manifests for amd64, arm64, and other architectures under a single tag. Kubernetes nodes pull the manifest matching their architecture automatically.
instruction in a Dockerfile adds a layer. Images with many thin layers incur more HTTP round trips during pull than equivalent images with fewer, larger layers.
Deleted files still occupy space in earlier layers.
layer adds 200 MB and a subsequent layer runs
, the 200 MB is still present in the first layer — the total image size includes both layers.
can point to different digests over time. Pulling
on different nodes can result in different images running in the same Deployment unless digest pinning is used.

When a Pod is scheduled to a node that has never run the image before, the full pull must complete before the container starts. Large images (several GiB) introduce significant scheduling latency that can violate service-level objectives.
If the registry is unreachable and
is set, Pods on new nodes will fail to start even if the image is already cached elsewhere in the cluster.
An OCI image consists of three artifact types, each stored as a separate blob in the registry, identified by its SHA-256 digest:

The manifest is the entry point of an OCI image. It is a JSON document that declares the image's media type, the digest of the config blob, and an ordered array of layer descriptors (each with a digest, size, and media type). The manifest itself is identified by its digest — this is the
) are mutable pointers to a manifest digest stored in the registry.
The config blob is a JSON document containing the image's build-time metadata: environment variables (

), exposed ports, labels, working directory, target OS and architecture, and the diff IDs of each layer (uncompressed SHA-256 digests used to build the chain ID for the overlay). The container runtime reads the config to set up the container's initial environment before starting the process.
Each layer is a compressed tar archive (

) that describes a set of filesystem changes: files added, modified, or deleted (whiteout files mark deletions). Layers are ordered — the first layer provides the base OS filesystem; each subsequent layer applies a diff on top. The runtime unpacks them in order, then mounts all read-only layers together via the kernel's overlayFS driver to produce a single unified filesystem view.
When a container is started, the runtime adds one more layer: the

(upperdir in overlayFS terminology). All writes by the running process go to this layer. The underlying image layers are never modified — they remain shared across all containers running from the same image on that node.
For multi-architecture images, the registry stores an
(formerly called a manifest list) at the tag — a JSON document that lists platform-specific manifest digests. When containerd on an arm64 node pulls
, it fetches the Image Index, finds the manifest for
, and pulls only that manifest's layers. The amd64 layers are never downloaded.

The diagram below shows the full OCI image pull flow: from the registry's content-addressed storage (manifest, config, layers), through containerd's pull and verification step, to the overlayFS mount that produces the container's unified rootfs.

attrs.alt: OCI image format architecture: registry layers, pull flow through containerd, overlayFS mount, and running container

content.text: OCI images are the only image format Kubernetes supports natively. Every workload that runs in a cluster — whether a Deployment, Job, CronJob, DaemonSet, or StatefulSet — specifies an OCI image for each container. The image format considerations that matter for operators are:
) for development convenience; use digest pinning (
for stable tags to avoid redundant pulls; use
only when you need the registry to be authoritative on every Pod start (useful with mutable tags in CI pipelines).
Structure Dockerfiles so slow-changing layers (base OS, runtime) come before fast-changing layers (application code). Node-level caching means unchanged layers are never re-pulled on image updates.
When your cluster has mixed-architecture nodes (ARM + amd64), build and push an Image Index so nodes automatically pull the correct architecture without separate image references.
Referencing Images in a Pod Spec

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx-demo
spec:
  containers:
  # By tag — convenient, but tag can be overwritten at registry
  - name: nginx-tag
    image: nginx:1.25
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

  # By digest — immutable; exact bytes guaranteed every pull
  - name: nginx-digest
    image: nginx@sha256:a3a7bbaa9c91e4e0d8e9e4d12e4d3c8f5a7e6b9c...  # truncated for readability
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

# Step 1: Create an image pull secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry my-registry-secret \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=myuser \
  --docker-password=mypassword

---
# Step 2: Reference it in the Pod spec
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: private-app
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:
  - name: my-registry-secret
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.example.com/myteam/app:2.1.0
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

# Inspect an image manifest locally (requires crictl or crane)
crictl inspecti nginx:1.25

# Or use crane (OCI registry client)
crane manifest nginx:1.25 | jq .
# {
#   "schemaVersion": 2,
#   "mediaType": "application/vnd.oci.image.manifest.v1+json",
#   "config": { "digest": "sha256:d7a45...", "size": 7682 },
#   "layers": [ ... ]
# }

# Resolve a tag to its digest
crane digest nginx:1.25
# sha256:a35e9fabd...

Multi-stage builds produce smaller images with fewer layers by separating build-time tools from the final runtime image. The resulting OCI image only contains layers needed to run the application — not layers needed to compile it.

# Stage 1: build (large — compiler, dev tools)
FROM golang:1.21 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o server ./cmd/server

# Stage 2: final image (small — only the binary)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
# Only two layers: distroless base + binary
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
USER nonroot:nonroot
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
# Resulting image: ~5 MB vs ~1 GB for the builder stage

# ❌ Bad layer order — dependencies reinstall on every code change
FROM node:20-alpine
COPY . .
RUN npm install
RUN npm run build

# ✅ Good layer order — node_modules cached until package.json changes
FROM node:20-alpine
# Copy only dependency files first
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production
# Copy source after; only this layer rebuilds on code changes
COPY src/ ./src/
RUN npm run build
produces the same layer digest as a previously cached layer (because
didn't change), containerd skips downloading that layer entirely during an image update. Only the application code layers are pulled.
Pinning Images to Digests in Production

# Resolve the tag to a digest before deploying
crane digest nginx:1.25
# sha256:a3a7bba...

# Pin the digest in the Deployment manifest
kubectl set image deployment/web \
  nginx=nginx@sha256:a3a7bba...

# In a Deployment spec, prefer digest over mutable tags
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    # Digest pinning: same bytes on every node, every pull, forever
    image: nginx@sha256:a3a7bba4c8f6d2b5e9a0c1d4f7e3b8a6c5d2e9f...
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent  # skip pull if digest is already cached

At Google-scale Kubernetes deployments, the gap between a warm node (image cached) and a cold node (image absent) can be the difference between a 2-second Pod startup and a 3-minute one for a large application image. Google's internal Kubernetes infrastructure uses

-based Image Streaming feature) — a technique where the container starts immediately using lazy-fetched layers over a FUSE filesystem, downloading only the bytes actually accessed by the process rather than pulling the entire image first.

For most teams, the practical equivalent is optimizing image size and layer structure: small images pull fast; well-structured layers maximize cache hits. A company running 500 microservices, each deploying multiple times per day, can eliminate gigabytes of redundant network traffic by ensuring all services share the same base OS layer (same digest, cached once per node) and structure their application layers above it.

# Check what images are cached on a node
ssh node-1 crictl images
# IMAGE                            TAG     IMAGE ID       SIZE
# docker.io/library/nginx          1.25    a3a7bba...     68.9MB
# registry.k8s.io/pause            3.9     e6f181...      744kB
# my-registry/app-backend          2.1.0   f5c9d8...      42.3MB

# Inspect layer details for an image
ssh node-1 crictl inspecti nginx:1.25 | jq '.info.imageSpec.layers'

# See total disk used by images on a node
ssh node-1 crictl imagefsinfo

# ❌ :latest is mutable — different nodes may pull different images
containers:
- name: app
  image: myapp:latest
  imagePullPolicy: Always   # double problem: forces pull on every Pod start

# ✅ Immutable tag or digest
containers:
- name: app
  image: myapp:2.1.0        # explicit version tag
  # or better:
  image: myapp@sha256:abc...
  imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
Storing secrets or tokens inside an image layer
Because image layers are immutable and stored in registries, any secret committed to a layer (even a later

instruction does not remove it from the earlier layer) is permanently visible to anyone with read access to the image. Secrets must never be baked into image layers; they belong in Kubernetes Secrets or external vaults, injected at runtime.

# ❌ Secret baked into a layer — permanently exposed even after deletion
FROM node:20
COPY .env .env           # .env contains DB_PASSWORD — now in the layer!
RUN npm install
RUN rm .env              # deleted in a later layer, but still in the COPY layer

# ✅ Never COPY secrets into a Dockerfile;
#    pass them as build args (still visible in history) or use multi-stage builds
#    with BuildKit secrets mounts:
# RUN --mount=type=secret,id=npmrc,target=/root/.npmrc npm install
Neglecting imagePullPolicy with IfNotPresent and mutable tags
If a CI pipeline pushes a new image under the same mutable tag (e.g.,
, nodes that already have an older version of
cached will not pull the update. The cluster can end up running different versions of the image on different nodes simultaneously, making debugging chaotic.

# ❌ Mutable tag + IfNotPresent = stale images on cached nodes
image: myapp:staging
imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent

# ✅ Option A: use Always pull with a mutable CI tag
image: myapp:staging
imagePullPolicy: Always

# ✅ Option B (better for production): immutable tag from CI
# e.g. myapp:commit-abc1234 or myapp@sha256:...
Pin images by digest in production manifests.
to guarantee the same bytes on every pull. Tools like Renovate can automate digest updates when a new tag is released.
Order Dockerfile layers from slowest- to fastest-changing.
Base OS → runtime dependencies → application code. This maximizes layer cache hits at both build time and on Kubernetes nodes.
Use multi-stage builds to minimize final image size.
Build tools, test dependencies, and intermediate artifacts should never appear in the production image. Use
to transfer only the output binary or compiled assets.
Use distroless or scratch base images for compiled binaries.
A Go or Rust binary has no runtime dependencies; a distroless or scratch-based image removes everything else (shell, package manager, libc), shrinking the attack surface and image size significantly.
Consolidate RUN instructions to reduce layer count.
Chain package installs, cleanup, and configuration into a single
instruction so deleted files do not occupy space in earlier layers.
Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent with immutable tags.
This avoids redundant registry calls on every Pod start while still ensuring nodes pull new images when the tag or digest changes.
Build and push multi-arch Image Indexes.
to produce a single tag that nodes on any architecture can pull correctly.

Image pull latency is one of the most common causes of slow Pod startup. On a Kubernetes node that has never seen an image before (a cold pull), the startup sequence is: schedule Pod → pull image → create container → run. For a 1 GiB image over a typical datacenter link (1 Gbps), the download alone takes about 8 seconds. Uncompress, verify, and mount add more time.

Reducing image size reduces cold pull time linearly. Layer caching at the node level means warm pulls (updating one application layer on an image already in cache) download only the changed layers — often just a few MB. Well-structured images with stable base layers and small, focused application layers reduce typical warm pull times to under a second.

At cluster scale, a large deployment event (rolling out a new image version across 500 nodes simultaneously) can overload a single registry instance. Mitigations include: running a in-cluster registry mirror or pull-through cache (e.g., Harbor, Docker Registry configured as a pull-through cache), configuring node-level image caching policies, or using P2P image distribution tools like Dragonfly or Kraken that turn nodes into a BitTorrent-like distribution network for large images.
The default CRI runtime for most Kubernetes distributions (kubeadm, GKE, EKS, AKS). Handles image pull, storage (content store), and container lifecycle. Inspect images via
CLI tools for inspecting, copying, and querying OCI images and registries without a running Docker daemon. Useful for CI pipelines that need to resolve digest pins or copy images between registries.
The default Docker build backend since Docker 23. Supports advanced features: secret mounts (
), SSH agent forwarding, and cross-platform builds. Produces OCI-conformant images.

An open-source OCI registry with Helm chart support, vulnerability scanning (via Trivy), image signing, and pull-through caching. Commonly deployed inside Kubernetes clusters to reduce external registry dependency.
Vulnerability scanners that analyze OCI image layers to detect packages with known CVEs. Both can run in CI pipelines to block deployment of vulnerable images before they reach the cluster.

A tool for signing OCI images and attaching signatures as OCI artifacts to the same registry tag. Kubernetes admission controllers (Connaisseur, Kyverno with Cosign) can verify signatures at admission time, preventing unsigned images from running.
Convenient for development; avoid :latest in production
GiB-scale; slow to start; no layer sharing; used for nodes, not workloads
Docker images are a subset of OCI; all major registries accept both
The OCI image format's content-addressable design provides a foundation for supply-chain security, but several additional controls are needed in production:
Sign images with Cosign (Sigstore) and enforce signature verification at admission using Kyverno or OPA/Gatekeeper policies. This prevents unsigned or tampered images from running in the cluster.
Run Trivy or Grype as a CI step before pushing. Block images with critical CVEs from reaching the production registry. Re-scan periodically: new CVEs are published against old images daily.
The OCI config specifies the user the container process runs as. Set
in the Dockerfile to a non-root UID and enforce it in Kubernetes via
Since OCI image layers are already read-only and all writes go to the per-container upperdir, setting
forces the container to mount its rootfs read-only, preventing an attacker from writing binaries or scripts to the container filesystem.
Configure a cluster-local registry mirror to ensure Pods can start even if the external registry is unreachable. Set
so nodes with cached images can restart Pods without a registry round trip during outages.
Think of an OCI image as a recipe book for building a filesystem. The
is the table of contents — it lists all the chapters (layers) and their page counts (sizes and digests). The
is the introduction — it describes how to set up the kitchen before cooking (environment variables, the default command). Each

is a chapter — it lists only the changes made to the filesystem at that step. When you run the recipe (start a container), all chapters are read in order to reconstruct the full filesystem, a blank writing pad (the writable layer) is placed on top, and the process starts cooking.
The key insight is that layers are

: if 50 containers on the same node use the same base OS layer, the bytes for that layer are stored only once. Writes by any one container only affect that container's private writing pad — they never reach the shared chapters. This is what makes containers lightweight: you only pay the disk cost of what is unique to each container, not a full copy of the filesystem for every instance.
An OCI image has three artifact types: manifest, config, and layers — all content-addressed by SHA-256 digest.
The manifest is the entry point; it references the config and the ordered list of layer blobs.
Image layers are immutable, shared, and deduplicated.
A node stores each unique layer exactly once. All containers referencing the same layer read the same bytes — the per-container writable upperdir holds their writes.
Tags are mutable pointers; digests are immutable identifiers.
) in production to guarantee reproducibility across all nodes and all time.
imagePullPolicy controls when Kubernetes pulls from the registry.
(default for versioned tags) skips pulls for cached images;
requires the image to be pre-loaded.
Put slow-changing layers (base OS, runtime) before fast-changing ones (app code). Unchanged layers are never re-pulled during updates.
Multi-stage builds eliminate build-time artifacts from the final image.
Only the runtime binary and its minimal dependencies belong in the image shipped to production.
Image security requires signing, scanning, and minimal base images.
Content-addressable digests verify integrity but not origin. Cosign signatures and admission-time verification close the supply-chain trust gap.
